title: 8-Hour Rigger Competency Slide Course
description: Classroom slide course for an 8-hour rigger program — regulations, WLL/design factor, inspection, rigging math, below-the-hook, and lift planning. Aligned with BC Crane Safety and WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15.

slides.title: 8-hour rigger competency program
slides.summary: Classroom slide course separate from reading lessons.
bullets: Designed for in-person delivery: clicker, TV cast, phone, offline save.
Roughly one slide every 5 minutes, plus breaks.
Reading depth lives in /lessons — slides teach field competencies.

slides.title: The Rules
slides.summary: Law first — then adopted standards and manufacturer limits.

items.label: WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 14 — cranes & material handling
WorkSafeBC OHSR Part 15 — rigging & qualified workers
B30.3 tower cranes · B30.5 mobile cranes

slides.title: Course map — nine blocks
bullets: Regulations & standards → ratings → protection → inspection.
Rigging math (largest block) → below-the-hook → lift planning → critical lifts.
Use /slides/charts for weight tables during math.

slides.title: Presenter tools
slides.summary: How to teach with this deck.
bullets: Arrow keys / Page Up-Down / Space / clicker remote.
Monitor icon casts audience view; Save offline before site without internet.
Full titles: tap Show full title on phone.

slides.title: OHSR Part 14 & 15 framework
bullets: Part 14 — material handling and cranes; Part 15 — rigging detail.
BCCSA guidance supports but does not replace regulation.
ASME B30.9 slings, B30.26 hardware, B30.5 mobile cranes commonly referenced.

slides.title: OHSR 15.2 — Qualified riggers
bullets: Rigging/slinging by or under direct supervision of a qualified worker.
Must know the specific rigging in use.
Must know Board-authorized code of signals.

slides.title: Qualified vs competent
bullets: Qualified: education, training, experience, understands hazards and controls.
Trainees rig only under direct supervision of a qualified rigger.
Employer verifies competency before assigning work.

slides.title: Employer & worker duties
bullets: Employer: safe workplace, maintained marked gear, procedures, training.
Worker: follow procedures, inspect gear, refuse unsafe work.
Supervisor: ensure compliance on the lift.

slides.title: Right to refuse unsafe work
bullets: Refuse work presenting undue hazard to self or others.
Damaged rigging, unknown weight, or no lift plan are examples.
Document and escalate — do not proceed under pressure.

slides.title: OHSR 15.3 — Land before detach
bullets: Loads must be safely landed and supported before unhooking.
Dropped loads during unhooking are preventable.
BCCSA key takeaway: land before detach.

slides.title: OHSR 15.4 — WLL not exceeded
bullets: Load on any rigging assembly must not exceed WLL.
Weakest component governs the assembly.
Verify before the operator takes the load.

slides.title: OHSR 15.5 — Identification
bullets: Fittings show manufacturer ID, product ID, and WLL.
Unmarked legacy gear: qualified person rates or remove from service.
Missing tags on slings — do not use.

slides.title: Makeshift fittings prohibited
bullets: OHSR 15.32 — makeshift fittings prohibited.
Bolts for shackle pins, homemade links, unknown hardware — reject.
Use rated, identified components only.

slides.title: Standards stack
bullets: Law (OHSR) → standards (CSA/ASME) → manufacturer → site procedure.
Engineered lift plans override routine picks when required.
When in conflict, most protective requirement applies.

slides.title: Documentation & due diligence
bullets: Inspection records, lift plans, competency records.
FLHA before task; toolbox talk for complex picks.
Supports employer due diligence under WorkSafeBC.

slides.title: Working Load Limit (WLL)
slides.summary: Maximum rated load for a configuration.
bullets: WLL is the maximum load the manufacturer assigns for that hook-up.
Changes with hitch type, angle, and termination.
Never exceed WLL on any component in the path.

slides.title: WLL vs SWL vs rated capacity
bullets: BC OHSR uses WLL on rigging hardware.
Crane charts use rated capacity at radius — different limit.
Do not swap crane capacity for sling WLL.

slides.title: Breaking strength
bullets: Breaking strength is lab/ catalog ultimate value.
Not a working number — never lift to breaking strength.
Used with design factor to derive WLL.

slides.title: Design factor (OHSR 15.1, 15.6)
bullets: Design factor = breaking strength ÷ WLL (minimum values in Table 15-1).
Example: wire rope sling minimum DF = 5.
Rigging supporting workers: DF at least 10.

slides.title: Table 15-1 design factors
bullets: Wire rope sling: 5 · Alloy chain sling: 4 · Synthetic web: 5.
Engineered dedicated assembly may use reduced DF for that lift only.
Re-rate per Table 15-1 before continued general use.

slides.title: Termination efficiency
bullets: OHSR 15.21 — reduce WLL per Figure 15-2 efficiency unless manufacturer says otherwise.
Clips, turnback eyes, and swaged ends are not 100% efficient.
Include in assembly calculation.

slides.title: Sling angle capacity
bullets: OHSR 15.34 — account for angle; Table 15-3 reductions.
Low included angle between legs increases tension.
Math block covers sin θ and multipliers.

slides.title: Choker vs basket vs vertical
bullets: Vertical hitch: full rated WLL per leg (symmetric pick).
Choker: capacity reduction per manufacturer/table.
Basket: increased capacity — verify angle and WLL chart.

slides.title: Assembly is only as strong as weakest link
bullets: Shackle + sling + hook + beam — check every element.
Include hook block weight and below-the-hook device if in load.
If any piece is overloaded, stop and re-plan.

slides.title: Proof test vs working load
bullets: Proof test finds manufacturing defects — not daily working load.
Fold-back eyes and some slings need proof test records (15.24, 15.36).
WLL is the field operating limit.

slides.title: OHSR 15.39 — Sharp edges
bullets: Protect slings from sharp edges and corners.
Use pads, sleeves, wood, or engineered edge protection.
Unprotected edges cut synthetics and damage wire rope.

slides.title: Softeners and sling protection
bullets: Corner pads, magnetic pads, hose, timber — site-appropriate.
Protection must stay in place during the full lift.
Inspect protection for damage; replace if compressed or cut.

slides.title: Load surface vs sling surface
bullets: Rough load surfaces abrade slings during set-down and hoist.
Burr, weld, and flange edges are common failure points.
When unsure, add protection and slow the lift.

slides.title: Temperature & chemical exposure
bullets: OHSR 15.53–15.54 — heat and chemicals damage web slings.
Keep slings away from hot work unless rated.
Quarantine and inspect after exposure.

slides.title: OHSR 15.31 — Inspect before use
bullets: Inspect slings and hardware before each use.
Visual and functional — tags, pins, latches, distortion.
No inspection = no lift.

slides.title: Pre-use inspection steps
bullets: Clean enough to see damage; check tags and WLL.
Look for wear, cracks, bends, corrosion, broken wires, cuts.
Check pins secured, hooks latch, no unauthorized mods.

slides.title: Wire rope — 3-6 rule
bullets: Remove: 6+ random broken wires in one lay, OR 3+ in one strand in one lay.
Stationary/guyline: 3+ broken wires between connections.
Also reject kinking, birdcaging, heat/arc, diameter loss.

slides.title: Rotation-resistant rope
bullets: OHSR 15.26: 2 broken wires in 6 diameters, or 4 in 30 diameters.
Plus all 15.25 criteria.
Common on many mobile crane load lines.

slides.title: Hook rejection — 15.29
bullets: Cracks, deformation, excessive throat opening, damaged latch.
Wear at bearing points and pin holes.
Tag out — do not return without qualified review.

slides.title: Wire rope sling rejection
bullets: Kinks, crushing, birdcaging, broken wires, heat damage.
Fittings loose or distorted.
When in doubt, tag out.

slides.title: Chain sling rejection
bullets: Stretch, cracks, gouges, twisted or bent links.
Measure wear at bearing points — compare to limits.
Periodic inspection complements daily pre-use.

slides.title: Synthetic web & round slings
bullets: Cuts, burns, UV damage, broken stitches, chemical attack.
Missing or illegible WLL tag — remove from service.
Do not use nylon/web slings for sharp unprotected edges.

slides.title: Tag out and quarantine
bullets: Remove from crane area; destroy or lock away if condemned.
Report to supervisor; log in crane/rigging record.
Re-inspect after shock load, overload, or arc contact.

slides.title: Shackle pins & open hooks
bullets: Hooks need latch unless exempt; pins secured against rotation.
Never replace shackle pin with a bolt.
Wedge socket dead end secured (15.9).

slides.title: Storage — 15.37
bullets: Off ground, dry, away from chemicals and UV.
No kinks in wire rope; no crushing synthetics.
Inspection culture beats rush.

slides.title: Why rigging math matters
bullets: Low sling angles, offset COG, and dynamic loads overload gear.
Math supports OHSR compliance — not optional paperwork.
Lessons: Module 6, Appendix B.

slides.title: Regulatory: determine load weight
bullets: Rigger must know load weight and communicate to operator.
Guessing is not compliance.
Use drawings, scales, tables, or engineering.

slides.title: Weight charts reference
bullets: Material density, plate, beam lb/ft, lumber, plywood/OSB, pipe, conversions, sling angles.
Dropdown charts at /slides/charts — use on phone or projector.
Manufacturer data beats field estimates.

slides.title: Volume × density method
bullets: Volume (ft³) × density (lb/ft³) = weight (lb).
Steel ≈ 490 lb/ft³; concrete ≈ 150 lb/ft³.
Add rigging, dunnage, and absorbed water.

slides.title: Steel plate example
bullets: Plate 4 ft × 8 ft × ½ in thick.
Area 32 ft² × 20.4 lb/ft² (½ in) ≈ 653 lb.
Round up; add hardware and uncertainty.

slides.title: Wide-flange beam example
bullets: W14×30 at 40 ft long ≈ 30 × 40 = 1,200 lb steel only.
Add stiffeners, bolts, paint, and lifting device.
Use beam marking or steel handbook.

slides.title: Pipe weight example
bullets: Look up lb/ft for nominal size and schedule.
Weight = lb/ft × length in feet.
Chart: /slides/charts?chart=pipe

slides.title: Concrete volume example
bullets: Volume in yd³ × ~4,000 lb/yd³ (wet concrete order of magnitude).
Or ft³ × 150 lb/ft³ for estimating.
Always confirm with pour ticket or engineering.

slides.title: Unit conversions
bullets: 1 kg ≈ 2.205 lb · 1 tonne ≈ 2,205 lb.
1 ft = 0.305 m — keep units consistent in one calculation.
Chart: /slides/charts?chart=conversions

slides.title: Include rigging in total suspended weight
bullets: Load + below-the-hook device + block + rigging weight (as applicable).
OHSR 15.60 — device may be part of lifted load.
Communicate total to operator.

slides.title: Sling tension introduction
bullets: Each leg carries more than half the load when angle drops.
Symmetric two-leg model is the teaching baseline.
Unequal legs covered later in this block.

slides.title: Sling tension formula
bullets: T = W ÷ (2 sin θ)
T = tension per leg · W = load weight · θ = angle from horizontal
Match calculator to procedure (DEG mode).

slides.title: Calculator: DEG and SIN
bullets: Set calculator to DEG (degrees), not RAD.
Practice on phone before the test lift.

slides.title: Leg angle vs included angle
bullets: θ in T = W/(2 sin θ) is each leg angle from horizontal.
Included angle between legs = 2θ only in symmetric bridle.
30° leg angle = 60° included — verify which convention your site uses.

slides.title: 90° from horizontal
bullets: Each leg carries W/2 in symmetric vertical-bridle model.
Steepest practical angle — lowest tension per leg.
Still verify WLL of each component.

slides.title: 60° from horizontal
bullets: T ≈ W × 1.155 ÷ 2 per leg in multiplier shorthand.
Common rigging angle — still acceptable with rated gear.

slides.title: 45° from horizontal
bullets: 15% more tension than vertical per multiplier table.
Watch horizontal compression on the load.

slides.title: 30° from horizontal
bullets: sin 30° = 0.5 → T = W — each leg equals full load weight!
Avoid unless engineered; extreme horizontal force.
BCCSA: select correctly rated components.

slides.title: Worked example: 10,000 lb @ 45°
bullets: Each sling and shackle must exceed 7,070 lb WLL in this hitch.
Round up; add dynamic factor per site policy.

slides.title: Worked example: 10,000 lb @ 30°
bullets: A 5-ton sling is not enough at 30° for a 5-ton load.
Re-rig steeper or use a spreader bar.

slides.title: Triangle geometry in rigging
bullets: Sling leg, horizontal, and vertical form a force triangle.
Shallow angle lengthens the sling leg vector.
Use site rigging cards to match angle convention.

slides.title: Horizontal compression
bullets: Legs push inward on the load — can crush or slide.
Use spreader bar to increase included angle.
Blocking and load strength matter.

slides.title: Center of gravity basics
bullets: COG is where the load balances in all axes.
Lift point should be above COG for stability.
Load tilts toward heavy side when COG is offset.

slides.title: Offset center of gravity
bullets: Shorten sling on heavy side or use adjustable gear.
Load may level when COG is under hook — verify before full hoist.
Tag line to control rotation.

slides.title: Complex / multi-part COG
bullets: Find COG per drawing or calculation; test with trial hoist low.
Machinery, vessels, and skids often hide internal mass.
Engineered pick points when COG is uncertain.

slides.title: Non-symmetrical two-leg loading
bullets: Legs at different angles or lengths do not share 50/50.
Heavier side and geometry determine each leg load.
Use engineered calculation or conservative assumptions.

slides.title: Three-leg bridle
bullets: One leg may go slack; two legs carry most of the load.
Design for uneven sharing unless engineered.
Four-point picks need similar caution.

slides.title: Choker hitch capacity
bullets: Choker reduces effective WLL — follow manufacturer table.
Choke angle at choke point affects rating.
Never choke on unprotected sharp edges.

slides.title: Basket hitch capacity
bullets: Basket can increase WLL if both legs share vertically.
Included angle and balance affect real capacity.
Still check angle tension on each leg.

slides.title: Table 15-3 awareness
bullets: OHSR Table 15-3 — WLL reductions for slings at angle.
Use with manufacturer charts.
Chart mirror: /slides/charts?chart=sling-angle

slides.title: Dynamic and shock loading
bullets: Swing, snag, sudden stop multiply forces.
Start lifts smoothly; control tag lines.
Site policy may require extra capacity margin.

slides.title: Load moment awareness
bullets: Moment = force × horizontal distance.
Rigger awareness: farther radius reduces crane capacity.
Coordinate with operator on radius and path.

slides.title: Communicate weight to operator
bullets: State calculated weight, rigging weight, and COG notes.
Operator confirms against chart and plan.
Stop if numbers do not match.

slides.title: When to escalate to engineering
bullets: Unknown COG, multi-crane, high value, or tight tolerance picks.
PE-stamped lift plan and rigging design.
Module 15 critical lift triggers.

slides.title: Practice: pick the sling
bullets: Load 8,000 lb, two-leg bridle at 45° from horizontal.
T ≈ 8,000 ÷ 1.414 ≈ 5,657 lb per leg — need sling WLL above that.
Add margin; check shackles and hooks too.

slides.title: Math block review
bullets: Determine weight · convert units · sling tension · COG · hitch type.
Charts at /slides/charts — keep open on second screen.
Reading: Module 6 + Appendix B.

slides.title: OHSR 15.57–15.60 overview
bullets: Standards apply to spreader bars, beams, clamps, magnets, etc.
WLL marked; device weight may count in load (15.60).
Do not exceed device WLL.

slides.title: Spreader bars
bullets: Reduce leg tension on wide loads.
Bar takes compression; slings to bar and hook.
Inspect lugs, pins, and welds; verify WLL.

slides.title: Lifting beams vs spreader bars
bullets: Lifting beam bends — top rigging carries bending.
Spreader bar is primarily compression.
Use correct device for the load and pick points.

slides.title: WLL and device weight
bullets: Include bar/beam weight in crane load when required.
Markings must match configuration in use.
PE stamp or engineering for non-standard picks.

slides.title: Plate clamps & magnets
bullets: Rated for plate thickness and orientation.
Test pull where procedure requires.
Never use damaged or unmarked clamps.

slides.title: Inspection of BTH devices
bullets: Welds, lugs, pins, pads, and WLL labels.
Remove if cracked, bent, or overloaded history.
Storage to prevent damage.

slides.title: Pick points & welding
bullets: Field-welded pick points need PE approval.
Alloy chain must not be welded.
Use engineered lugs where possible.

slides.title: Pre-lift planning elements
bullets: Weight, COG, path, landing zone, weather, personnel, rigging, crane config.
FLHA and toolbox review.
Lesson: Module 15.

slides.title: Hazards & controls
bullets: Powerlines, ground, swing, pinch points, public interface.
Tag lines, exclusion zones, spotters.
Stop work when conditions change.

slides.title: Lift path & landing zone
bullets: Clear route from pick to set; no personnel under load.
Landing area prepared; cribbing ready.
Escape routes for riggers.

slides.title: Communication plan
bullets: Hand signals per Figure 15-1 when used.
One authoritative signal person when required.
Radio: dedicated frequency for tower (OHSR 14.49).

slides.title: Roles: rigger, operator, signaler
bullets: Rigger: rigging integrity and load control at hook.
Operator: crane functions per signals.
Signaler: clear view and standard signals.

slides.title: Documentation
bullets: Lift plan sketch, weight calc, equipment list, signatures.
Critical lifts need engineered plan on file.
Brief crew before first pick.

slides.title: Routine vs planned lifts
bullets: Routine: mental check + FLHA may suffice per employer.
Non-routine: written plan minimum.
When triggers met → critical lift process.

slides.title: Critical lift triggers
bullets: High consequence, unusual weight/shape, multi-crane, tight tolerance.
Over public, live plant, or engineered exclusion from routine.
Employer/site policy may add triggers.

slides.title: Engineered lift plan
bullets: Stamped drawings, rigging arrangement, crane config, sequence.
Lift director coordinates execution.
No improvisation on critical picks.

slides.title: Multi-crane awareness
bullets: Load sharing, synchronization, wind, communication.
Always engineered — not field rigged.
Reading: Module 11 tandem lifts.

slides.title: 8-hour competency review
slides.summary: What you should carry to site.
bullets: Regulations · WLL/DF · inspection · math · BTH · planning.
Charts: /slides/charts · Depth: /lessons · Practice: /practice-test.
Land before detach · determine weight · protect edges.

slides.title: Field readiness
bullets: Practice calculator sling problems daily.
Pre-use inspect every shift.
In-person certification: written + practical evaluation.
